facts.label: THE KIMBERLEY KIT
facts.story: On 13 June 1978 in Mar del Plata, France and Hungary both turned up for their World Cup opener carrying only their white away kits. With kickoff delayed while officials scrambled, organizers raided the wardrobe of local amateur club Atlético Kimberley and handed France a set of green-and-white vertical stripes to wear instead. Les Bleus won 3–1 in borrowed colors belonging to nobody's flag, and a modest Argentine club became a permanent, improbable footnote in French football folklore.

facts.label: THE GALLIC ROOSTER
facts.story: France's crest carries a joke two thousand years old. Romans called the people of Gaul "Galli," a word that also meant rooster, and ancient satirists loved the pun. The bird first landed on a French football badge in 1907, wings spread beneath the letters of the sport's governing committee, and has anchored the shirt ever since. That's why Les Bleus line up at every World Cup with a crowing cockerel over their hearts — a Roman insult reborn as a national emblem.

1930.headline: First Goal in World Cup History
1930.story: On 13 July 1930 in Montevideo, Lucien Laurent turned in a cross to score the first goal in World Cup history, sparking a 4–1 win over Mexico that also featured Marcel Langiller and a brace from André Maschinot. Back-to-back 1–0 losses to Argentina and Chile then sent France home without another point.
1930.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts (FFF issue, pre-sponsor era)

1934.headline: One Match, Extra Time, Out
1934.story: France's entire 1934 tournament lasted a single afternoon. Playing straight knockout with no group stage, Les Bleus led through goals from Georges Verriest and Jean Nicolas but couldn't hold off Austria's famed 'Wunderteam,' who needed extra time to escape Colombes 3–2 and end France's World Cup before it truly began.

1938.headline: Hosts Fall to Defending Champions
1938.story: Hosting for the first time, France beat Belgium 3–1 behind Jean Nicolas's brace, then met reigning champions Italy in the quarter-final at the Stade Olympique de Colombes. Oscar Heisserer's strike briefly leveled the tie, but Italy's Silvio Piola and Gino Colaussi pulled away for a 3–1 win that sent the hosts home early.
1938.homeColors: blue shirt, white shorts, home-tournament ceremony kit (FFF issue)

1954.headline: Kopa Shines, Group Stage Ends It
1954.story: A young Raymond Kopa scored as France beat Mexico 3–2 in Geneva, a preview of the playmaker who'd soon join Real Madrid and lead France's golden generation. But an opening 1–0 loss to Yugoslavia, in a tournament that seeded rivals apart until late rounds, was enough to eliminate Les Bleus after just two matches.
1954.homeColors: blue shirt, white shorts (Le Coq Sportif)

1958.headline: Fontaine's Record 13 Goals
1958.story: Just Fontaine scored a still-unbroken 13 goals in a single World Cup, including a hat-trick past Paraguay and a record four in the third-place win over West Germany. Pelé's hat-trick ended France's run 5–2 in the semi-final, but Fontaine and Kopa had already carried Les Bleus to their best finish yet.
1958.homeColors: blue shirt, white shorts, red socks (Le Coq Sportif)

matches.scorers: Fontaine 24', Fontaine 30', Piantoni 52', Wisnieski 61', Fontaine 67', Kopa 70', Vincent 83'
Fontaine 16', Kopa 27', Fontaine 36', Douis 50', Fontaine 78', Fontaine 89'

1966.headline: Wembley Ends a Quiet Cup
1966.story: France drew Mexico and lost narrowly to Uruguay before meeting eventual champions England at Wembley in the group finale. Bobby Charlton's side won 2–0 without ever being seriously threatened, and Les Bleus went home from London without a single win, their quietest tournament of the decade.
1966.homeColors: blue shirt, white shorts, red socks (Le Coq Sportif, stripped-back design)

1978.headline: Borrowed Stripes, Group of Death
1978.story: Drawn into a brutal group with Argentina and Italy, a young Michel Platini's France beat Hungary 3–1 while wearing green-and-white stripes borrowed from a local Argentine club after a kit mix-up. It wasn't enough: consecutive 2–1 losses to Italy and the host nation eliminated France despite the win.
1978.homeColors: blue shirt, white shorts, red socks (Le Coq Sportif)
1978.awayColors: borrowed green-and-white vertical stripes from Club Atlético Kimberley (worn once, vs Hungary, after a kit clash)

1982.headline: Seville's Unforgettable Heartbreak
1982.story: Michel Platini's France reached the semi-final in Seville, where Patrick Battiston was flattened by an uncarded challenge from goalkeeper Harald Schumacher. Les Bleus led 3–1 in extra time before West Germany equalized and won the World Cup's first-ever penalty shootout. A drained France then lost the third-place match to Poland 3–2.
1982.homeColors: blue shirt with white and red pinstripes, white shorts (Le Coq Sportif)

1986.headline: Platini's Penalty Redemption vs Brazil
1986.story: France avenged 1982 in the quarter-final, outlasting Brazil on penalties after Michel Platini missed in regulation but Luis Fernández buried the clincher. West Germany again ended the run in the semis, 0–2, before Les Bleus beat Belgium 4–2 in extra time to claim third place with the same golden generation.
1986.homeColors: blue shirt, white shorts, red socks (Adidas, first tournament under the new kit deal)

1998.headline: Zidane's Two Headers at Home
1998.story: On home soil at the Stade de France, Zinedine Zidane scored twice with headers off corners in the first half of the final, and Emmanuel Petit added a stoppage-time third as France routed holders Brazil 3–0. It was France's first World Cup title, capping a tournament in which they'd conceded just twice in seven matches.
1998.homeColors: blue shirt with a thick red central stripe and thin white stripes, white shorts (Adidas)
1998.awayColors: white shirt with reversed stripe pattern — blue stripe, red trim (Adidas)

2002.headline: Champions Exit Without Scoring
2002.story: Defending champions without an injured Zinedine Zidane for two matches, France lost their opener to World Cup debutants Senegal, drew Uruguay 0–0, then fell to Denmark 0–2. Les Bleus became the first defending champions since Brazil in 1966 to exit in the group stage — and did it without scoring a single goal.
2002.homeColors: blue shirt with tonal detailing, white shorts (Adidas, defending-champions kit)

2006.headline: The Headbutt Final
2006.story: Zinedine Zidane's cheeky Panenka penalty opened the final before Marco Materazzi equalized, and in extra time Zidane head-butted Materazzi in the chest and was sent off in what would be his final match. Italy won the shootout 5–3, denying France a second title in Zidane's last game for Les Bleus.
2006.awayColors: white shirt with a gradient running from light blue to bright red across the chest (Adidas)

2010.headline: The Knysna Mutiny
2010.story: France's campaign collapsed off the pitch before it did on it: after Nicolas Anelka was sent home for insulting coach Raymond Domenech at halftime, players staged a training boycott at their Knysna base. Winless in three matches and beaten by host South Africa, Les Bleus finished bottom of their group.
2010.homeColors: blue shirt with red trim, white shorts (Adidas)
2010.awayColors: white shirt with red and blue alternating pinstripes and gold trim (Adidas)

2014.headline: Steady Run Ends in Rio
2014.story: Karim Benzema scored twice in a 3–0 opener over Honduras and Les Bleus routed Switzerland 5–2, restoring credibility after 2010's collapse. A Mats Hummels header at the Maracanã ended the run 1–0 to eventual champions Germany in the quarter-final, France's deepest run since 2006.
2014.homeColors: dark navy shirt with white collar, white shorts (Nike)
2014.awayColors: white shirt with subtle horizontal stripes and a three-button collar (Nike)

2018.headline: Mbappé Announces a Generation
2018.story: A 19-year-old Kylian Mbappé scored twice in a 4–3 thriller over Argentina, then struck again in the 4–2 final win over Croatia, becoming only the second teenager after Pelé to score in a World Cup final. Antoine Griezmann and Paul Pogba also found the net as France claimed its second title.
2018.homeColors: dark blue shirt with light blue soundwave print on the sleeves, white shorts (Nike)

2022.headline: Mbappé's Final Hat-Trick, Still Lost
2022.story: Down 2–0 with 10 minutes left in the final, Kylian Mbappé scored three times in a stunning comeback, forcing extra time against Argentina in one of the greatest finals ever played. Lionel Messi's Argentina held on to win the shootout 4–2, leaving Mbappé a hat-trick scorer on the losing side.
2022.homeColors: dark navy shirt with metallic gold detailing, white shorts (Nike)
2022.awayColors: white shirt with blue trim and a Toile de Jouy-inspired pattern celebrating French landmarks (Nike)
